abstract: We present a keyword-based query interface for knowledge bases—including relational and deductive databases—that leverages contextual background knowledge such as join conditions and synonyms to interpret user intent. In our framework, join paths can be identified via existing foreign key constraints or, in cases where schema constraints are absent, learned from historical query patterns. By transforming a simple list of keywords into a structured logical term, our approach bridges the gap between natural language and formal database queries. The core of this system is an intelligent, cooperative parsing mechanism that hypothesizes the underlying semantics of a word list and reconstructs missing links between concepts using database metadata. We implement this via an extended version of Definite Clause Grammars (DCG) that dynamically interrogates the database schema to annotate tokens as table names, attributes, values, or relationships. Our tool, DDQL, translates these keyword inputs into a set of candidate queries expressed in an extended Datalog-based domain-specific language, allowing users to select the interpretation that best matches their information needs.
